medications.indication_rationale: Oral doxycycline at a low, sub-antimicrobial dose is first-line for a moderate-to-severe papulopustular rosacea flare — it works through a potent anti-inflammatory effect, NOT by killing bacteria (the pustules are sterile). It requires a prescriber, so the telehealth AI Doctor cannot supply it directly and must arrange a GP / telehealth-prescriber review to commence it, rather than prescribing autonomously.
medications.dose_route_frequency: doxycycline 50 mg orally once daily (sub-antimicrobial anti-inflammatory dose), review at 4 weeks
contraindications_in_this_case: Pregnancy and breastfeeding (tetracyclines contraindicated) — confirm before prescribing
Children under 8 years — not applicable to this adult patient

medications.indication_rationale: Topical metronidazole or ivermectin is a first-line maintenance therapy for papulopustular rosacea and is typically added or continued after the acute flare settles; the source note plans this at the 4-week review. Requires a prescriber, so it is arranged via GP review, not supplied by the AI Doctor.
medications.dose_route_frequency: apply thinly to affected central face once to twice daily as directed, commenced/continued at GP review

medications.indication_rationale: A topical corticosteroid must NOT be used — although it transiently soothes the redness, it drives steroid-induced/peri-oral rosacea and worsens the condition. Recommending one is a scored error of commission.

The pustules are STERILE inflammatory lesions, not an infection — treating with an anti-staphylococcal antibiotic for presumed facial cellulitis/staph infection is inappropriate and reflects the infection over-call trap. Antibacterial cover is only warranted if genuine cellulitis features develop.

medications.drug_name: topical benzoyl peroxide / comedolytic acne therapy
medications.indication_rationale: Acne therapies such as benzoyl peroxide are irritant on a compromised rosacea barrier and are not indicated — the absence of comedones excludes acne. Using them would aggravate the flare.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Immediately cease all harsh exfoliating scrubs and acid toners; switch to a gentle non-foaming cream cleanser and a plain fragrance-free moisturiser to repair the skin barrier.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Over-exfoliation has compromised the barrier and is driving the stinging and inflammation; barrier repair rapidly reduces the burning.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Use cool compresses on the face for the acute burning sensation.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Simple, safe symptomatic relief for the neurovascular burning while anti-inflammatory therapy takes effect.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Identify and avoid personal flare triggers — sun exposure, alcohol, heat and spicy food — and use daily broad-spectrum SPF and a physical hat for photoprotection.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Trigger avoidance and photoprotection prevent the vasodilatory flushing that provokes flares and is the cornerstone of long-term rosacea control.

patient_education_points.point: This is a flare of a benign, chronic inflammatory skin condition — the pus-topped spots are sterile inflammation, NOT a bacterial infection eating into the face.
The low-dose antibiotic that a GP may prescribe is used for its anti-inflammatory effect, not to kill bacteria; it settles the redness and bumps over 2-4 weeks.
The condition cannot be permanently cured but is very well controlled with gentle skincare, trigger avoidance and medication.
Do not scrub, pick at, or apply strong acids/steroid creams to the face — these worsen rosacea.

follow_up_plan.interval: GP (or telehealth-prescriber) review within a few days to commence oral therapy; routine dermatology-informed GP review at 4 weeks to assess response and add topical therapy
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Response of erythema and papulopustules to therapy, barrier recovery, tolerance of doxycycline, and any emerging ocular involvement; commence topical metronidazole/ivermectin.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: New eye redness/grittiness/pain or visual change; the face becoming hard, hot and swollen with fever; or the redness spreading with a sharply demarcated advancing border.

must_include_items: Recognise this as a benign inflammatory facial flare (rosacea spectrum), NOT a facial cellulitis or bacterial infection
Reassure the patient and explicitly de-escalate the infection fear without over-triaging to ED
Advise immediate cessation of harsh exfoliating scrubs/acid toners and a switch to gentle cleanser + plain moisturiser (barrier repair)
Advise cool compresses for the burning and trigger avoidance (sun, alcohol, heat, spicy food) with photoprotection
Arrange a routine GP / telehealth-prescriber review to consider sub-antimicrobial-dose oral doxycycline and topical anti-inflammatory therapy
Safety-net for ocular rosacea (red/gritty/painful eyes, visual change) and for evolving true cellulitis (fever, hard hot spreading demarcated swelling)
acceptable_alternatives: Recommending a pharmacist review or GP appointment for the prescription rather than a specific drug name, provided recognition + self-care + safety-net are correct
Deferring the choice between topical metronidazole and ivermectin to the prescriber
errors_of_omission: Failing to advise cessation of the harsh scrubs/acid toners
Failing to safety-net for ocular rosacea
Failing to safety-net for evolving facial cellulitis
Failing to arrange any GP follow-up for definitive therapy
errors_of_commission: Over-triaging to the emergency department for presumed facial cellulitis / staphylococcal infection
Recommending an anti-staphylococcal antibiotic (e.g. flucloxacillin) for the sterile pustules
Recommending a topical corticosteroid (drives steroid-induced rosacea)
Treating as acne with benzoyl peroxide / comedolytics
Autonomously issuing a specific prescription and dose rather than arranging a prescriber review
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass REQUIRES recognising the benign flare and NOT over-triaging to ED, PLUS giving barrier-repair/trigger-avoidance self-care AND safety-netting for ocular involvement and evolving cellulitis. Escalating a systemically-well, afebrile, bilateral, non-indurated facial rash to emergency care is the primary scored over-triage defect.
